text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPLE-PHASE)

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old female with history of moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head, status post neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles), concurrent chemoradiation with capecitabine, and open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV segmental resection and primary venous reconstruction (ypT3 N1 M0, R0 resection). Now approximately 8 months post-surgery, completing 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine. Surveillance imaging to evaluate for locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. CA 19-9 at time of scan: 19 U/mL. CEA: 2.0 ng/mL.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in the arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), portal venous, and delayed phases following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (iohexol). Multiplanar reconstructions (axial, coronal, sagittal) and curved planar reformations of the mesenteric vasculature and biliary system were generated. Oral contrast was not administered. No significant motion artifact. Technically adequate examination.

COMPARISON: Prior CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol obtained pre-surgically (approximately 8 months prior), at which time the primary mass measured 2.3 cm. Immediate post-operative imaging not available for direct comparison.

FINDINGS:

SURGICAL BED / PANCREATIC REMNANT:
The operative bed in the right upper quadrant demonstrates expected post-pancreaticoduodenectomy appearances. There is a small remnant of the pancreatic body and tail, with the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis identifiable as a focal area of soft tissue thickening and staple artifact at the expected location of the pancreatic neck reconstruction, measuring approximately 1.8 cm in transverse dimension. This region is stable in appearance when compared with the pre-operative baseline and demonstrates no suspicious enhancement pattern, no discrete soft tissue mass, and no perianastomotic fluid collection or abscess. Mild, diffuse fatty replacement and volume loss of the pancreatic remnant is noted, consistent with known exocrine insufficiency and post-surgical atrophy. The main pancreatic duct of the remnant is mildly dilated at approximately 4 mm, likely related to anastomotic fibrosis rather than obstructive recurrence given the overall stable soft tissue appearance and normal tumor markers. No focal enhancing nodule is identified at or near the pancreaticojejunostomy.

A thin, curvilinear band of soft tissue density is present in the right retroperitoneum and the porta hepatis region, encircling the hepaticojejunostomy and extending along the expected course of prior dissection planes. This is consistent with post-surgical fibrosis and desmoplastic change. There is no associated mass effect on adjacent structures, no regional arterial encasement, and no progressive enlargement relative to pre-operative imaging. No rim-enhancing collection to suggest a late post-operative fluid collection.

VASCULAR RECONSTRUCTION:
The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) demonstrates normal caliber and opacification throughout, including at the site of the previously performed segmental resection and end-to-end anastomosis. The anastomotic segment is identifiable by a focal area of wall thickening and low-density surrounding fibrosis but shows no evidence of thrombosis, stenosis, or extrinsic compression. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is patent and freely visualized in its proximal and mid portions; the prior area of tumor abutment along the right lateral wall of the SMA is now replaced by linear soft tissue density consistent with scar tissue, without residual or recurrent tumor nodularity. The portal vein is widely patent. The celiac axis, splenic artery, and common hepatic artery are unremarkable.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
The hepaticojejunostomy is intact. There is no intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The biliary metallic stent previously placed for relief of malignant obstruction is no longer present (removed at the time of surgery), and the biliary anastomosis appears patent with no upstream biliary ductal dilatation. No pneumobilia to suggest biliary-enteric anastomotic dysfunction.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour and homogeneous parenchymal attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on any phase of contrast enhancement. In particular, no hypervascular or hypoenhancing nodules are present, no peripheral rim-enhancing lesions, and no diffuse infiltrative changes are seen. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent. No perihepatic fluid.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is present and unremarkable, measuring approximately 9.4 cm in greatest craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesions.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal nodule or mass is identified.

KIDNEYS:
Both kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal cortical lesion, hydronephrosis, or perinephric abnormality. The right kidney demonstrates mild cortical thinning at the upper pole, likely related to prior minor vascular territory ischemia from surgical manipulation, unchanged from prior imaging. No new renal lesions.

LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, or pelvis. Specifically, there are no enlarged nodes along the hepatoduodenal ligament, in the para-aortic or aortocaval regions, or at the celiac axis. Several small lymph nodes up to 7 mm are scattered within the mesentery and retroperitoneum; these are within normal size criteria and are morphologically benign in appearance (no central necrosis, no abnormal enhancement). No adenopathy in the gastrohepatic ligament or portacaval space.

PERITONEUM:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity, omental infiltration, or serosal implants are identified on any phase of imaging. The mesenteric fat is clear without stranding or soft tissue deposits to suggest carcinomatosis.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
The gastric remnant (post-Billroth II-type reconstruction) is without mucosal irregularity or wall thickening. The reconstructed jejunal loops at the hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, and duodenojejunostomy appear appropriately positioned without obstruction. The small bowel and visualized colon are unremarkable. No small bowel dilation or air-fluid levels. No pneumatosis.

CHEST (VISUALIZED LUNG BASES):
The visualized lung bases are clear. No pleural effusion. No suspicious pulmonary nodules identified within the imaged lung fields, though dedicated CT thorax would be required for comprehensive pulmonary evaluation.

BONY STRUCTURES:
The visualized osseous structures are without convincing lytic or blastic lesion. Moderate multilevel degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, age-appropriate.

IMPRESSION:

1. Status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV segmental resection and hepaticojejunostomy. The surgical bed demonstrates expected post-operative fibrotic change without evidence of locoregional tumor recurrence. No suspicious soft tissue mass, enhancing nodule, or abnormal collection in the operative bed.

2. The SMV venous reconstruction is patent without thrombosis or hemodynamically significant stenosis. The SMA is unencumbered at the prior site of tumor abutment, with residual fibrotic change only.

3. The liver is without focal lesions on multiphasic imaging. No features to suggest hepatic metastatic disease.

4. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, pathologic lymphadenopathy, or ascites.

5. Mild dilatation of the residual pancreatic duct remnant (approximately 4 mm), likely related to post-anastomotic fibrotic change in the setting of normal tumor markers; direct recurrence not favored but warrants continued surveillance.

6. Overall, in conjunction with the current serum CA 19-9 of 19 U/mL and CEA of 2.0 ng/mL, no imaging evidence of recurrent or metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is identified at this time.

Dictated and electronically signed by: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Section
